topic: Antarctic gravity hole — Cycle 3 change-driven review after post-hoc paragraph + refraction-cascade paragraph additions
change_summary: detail_evidence grew 69% (3,010 → 5,079 chars) with new refraction-cascade closing paragraph and expanded source citation (Pappa 2019 replaces Ebbing 2021). detail_verdict_text grew 203% (487 → 1,477 chars) with post-hoc boilerplate paragraph. TLDR fields added. Verdict unchanged (Self-Contradicted).
our_argument_summary: The core argument is unchanged from c2: the dome's own H(r) = 8,537·exp(−r/8,619) implies gravity variation ~170× larger than GRACE/Swarm measurements under any monotonic H→g coupling, and the dome's only quantitative gravity formula (monitor.py Domain 4) is a WGS84 identity at r=0. The c2 review's five recommended edits were all implemented: Section 2.2 → 2.3/2.8 cross-ref fix, tightened finding, anchored monitor.py citation (V51.1, gravity-anomaly domain), added the H^(1/5) bracketing example, and generalized 'under any mechanism where gravitational effects depend monotonically on H(r)'. Since c2, two new additions: (a) a closing refraction-cascade paragraph invoking n(r) at Antarctic rim radii, and (b) a second detail_verdict paragraph asserting this claim fails the post-hoc test. Verdict unchanged.

relevant_files: raw-text/02-wins.txt (dome V51.2 — WIN numbering stops at WIN-039 here; WIN-067 was added in V51.0 per commit history)
raw-text/08-live-power.txt (dome V51.2 live-domain dump — Domain 4 gravity formula + Domain 19 Mascon Gravity)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: Two gravity-relevant entries in the dome's live-domains file: Domain 4 'Equatorial Gravity' with formula g(r) = 9.7803×(1+0.005307×exp(−r/λg)) evaluated only at the equator (r=0), and Domain 19 'Mascon Gravity' with a bare falsification threshold 'Anomaly <20 mGal' and a correlation line 'Mascon Gravity ↔ SAA Boundary: r=0.92 ("Sub-terrestrial structure")'. No code fetches GRACE/Swarm anomaly values and compares them against a dome-derived prediction. Domain 19 is pass-by-threshold, not by-prediction.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Hardcoded. Domain 4 evaluates WGS84 normal gravity at r=0 and calls the agreement a confirmation. Domain 19 has no formula at all — just a threshold test, which any measurement of the East Antarctic anomaly will pass because the anomaly is real and exceeds 20 mGal.
data_sources_used: NOTE: no GRACE or Swarm fetch in the Mascon Gravity domain per our reading of live-power.txt V51.2
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The dome's V51.2 terminology is 'Sub-terrestrial structure,' not 'toroidal sump node' (see code_discrepancies below). Whatever the label, no published mechanism links dome geometry to the observed anomaly magnitude.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: The claim that monitor.py contains g(r) = 9.7803×(1+0.005307×exp(−r/8619)) matches raw-text/08-live-power.txt Domain 4 line-for-line. Mathematically re-verified: 9.7803×1.005307 = 9.832204 m/s², which matches the WGS84 polar normal-gravity value 9.8321864 m/s² to 0.0002%. However, our review's attribution 'toroidal sump node' does NOT appear anywhere in the current raw-text/ (V51.2) — the dome's own V51.2 terminology in Domain 19 is 'Sub-terrestrial structure' per raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 152. The c1 review explicitly flagged this concern ('search raw-text for sump / sump node'). The c2 verdict acknowledged the term is undefined but did not check whether the dome has since dropped it. If V51.0 (when WIN-067 was added) used 'toroidal sump node' and V51.2 moved to 'Sub-terrestrial structure,' our review is attacking stale terminology. This is adversarially significant — a defender can dismiss the paragraph by saying 'I don't use that term.'

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Domain 19 'Mascon Gravity' has a one-line falsification threshold ('Anomaly <20 mGal') with no data-fetch and no predicted value to compare against. Domain 4 evaluates g at r=0 only — a WGS84 identity. Tag matches c1 and c2.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: The Antarctic gravity low has been explained by standard geophysics (mantle plume + post-glacial isostatic adjustment) since at least Ferraccioli et al. 2011 and is now continuously monitored by GRACE-FO. The dome rebrands the feature without proposing an alternative mechanism, formula, or numerical prediction. Whether the label is 'toroidal sump node' (V51.0?) or 'Sub-terrestrial structure' (V51.2), the dome contributes no new physics. Tag matches c2.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: The Antarctic gravity anomaly has been continuously measured by GRACE since 2002 and published extensively (Pappa 2019, Forte 2010, Ferraccioli 2011) — all predate V51.0 (2026-04-05) where WIN-067 was introduced. Tag matches c2.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No derivation from dome geometry exists in the repository. Domain 4's exponential factor is decorative (collapses at r=0). Domain 19 is a bare threshold. The only number that 'works' (Domain 4's 9.8322 at r=0) is WGS84 expressed in dome notation. Tag matches c2.

holes_found.description: STALE TERMINOLOGY — 'TOROIDAL SUMP NODE' ATTRIBUTION NOT SUPPORTED BY V51.2 DOME TEXT. Our detail_claim ('confirms toroidal sump node'), tldr_evidence, tldr_verdict, and detail_verdict_text all cast the dome as 'introducing the undefined term toroidal sump node.' But the current dome (V51.2) raw-text uses 'Sub-terrestrial structure' in Domain 19 (raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 152: 'Mascon Gravity ↔ SAA Boundary: r=0.92 ("Sub-terrestrial structure")'). The phrase 'toroidal sump node' appears nowhere in the current raw-text (I grepped all 8 raw-text files and the v50.6 archive — 0 hits). Either WIN-067 was originally proposed with 'sump node' terminology in V51.0 and the dome author has since replaced it with 'Sub-terrestrial structure' (a reactive revision we would want to credit and pivot our attack to), OR our review invented the label. In either case, the current text lets a dome defender say 'I don't use that term; your review attacks a strawman' and walk away. The underlying self-contradiction (H(r) → 170× gravity variation) still holds regardless of label — but our most quotable rhetorical line ('undefined toroidal sump node') depends on a term the dome may no longer use.
holes_found.recommendation: Do ONE of the following: (a) locate the V51.0 or archive-pre-V51.2 dome page where 'toroidal sump node' is used, cite it explicitly with a quote + source + date so the attribution is bulletproof; (b) update detail_claim / tldrs / detail_verdict_text to attack the current V51.2 label 'Sub-terrestrial structure' with the same critique (still undefined, still no mechanism, still no formula linking dome geometry to anomaly magnitude); or (c) make the critique label-agnostic: 'Whatever the dome calls the mechanism (V51.0 "toroidal sump node," V51.2 "Sub-terrestrial structure"), no equation in the repository links dome geometry to the observed anomaly magnitude.' Option (c) is most robust to further dome revisions.

holes_found.description: QUANTITATIVE ERROR IN NEW REFRACTION-CASCADE PARAGRAPH. The closing sentence of detail_evidence says: 'at the Antarctic rim (r ≈ 17,000–20,015 km), the firmament ceiling H(r) ≈ 1,400–800 km.' Recomputed from the dome's own formula: H(17,000) = 8,537·exp(−17,000/8,619) = 1,188 km, NOT 1,400 km. The r-range and H-range given as endpoints are not self-consistent. H(r) = 1,400 km corresponds to r ≈ 15,583 km (not 17,000 km). H(r) = 800 km corresponds to r ≈ 20,406 km (close to, but past, the stated rim 20,015 km; H(20,015) = 837 km). An adversarial reader pulls out a calculator, finds the mismatch, and uses it to discredit the entire refraction-cascade paragraph — exactly the kind of precision gap that our review elsewhere criticizes the dome for. Two valid fixes exist: either widen r to 15,500–20,015 km (H ≈ 1,400–840 km) or tighten H to 1,190–840 km at r ≈ 17,000–20,015 km.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace with one of: 'at the Antarctic rim (r ≈ 15,500–20,015 km), the firmament ceiling H(r) ≈ 1,400–840 km' OR 'at the Antarctic rim (r ≈ 17,000–20,015 km), H(r) ≈ 1,190–840 km' — whichever matches the intended argument. Prefer the first, which gives a larger rim band and a cleaner round number.

holes_found.description: VAGUE CLOSING CLAIM WITHOUT QUANTITATIVE CONTENT. The refraction-cascade paragraph ends: 'The n(r) values implied at these radii would produce gravitational-index coupling effects catastrophically inconsistent with GRACE satellite gravimetry.' This is assertion without mechanism: (a) there is no established notion of 'gravitational-index coupling' in either standard physics or the dome's own writing — the term sounds authoritative but is not defined; (b) no numerical bound is given for how much the coupling should perturb GRACE measurements; (c) since GRACE orbits at ~490 km (well below the firmament), the question of how n(r) at r=17,000–20,015 km would affect spaceborne gravimetry requires a ray-path argument that is not supplied. A dome defender will ask 'What coupling? Show me the formula.' The paragraph was a strong addition up to this sentence; the sentence weakens it.
holes_found.recommendation: Either drop the closing sentence entirely (the paragraph works without it), or replace with a specific mechanism: 'A medium with n ≈ 1.2–2.8 on the optical path bends light by enough to move apparent planetary positions by degrees (see Section 1.8); whether the same medium influences inertial acceleration of orbiting test masses is an open question the dome does not address — but it is a question the dome must answer, because GRACE's 1-μGal sensitivity is orders of magnitude finer than any plausible self-cancellation.' Or: drop and leave the WGS84-tautology paragraph as the closer.

holes_found.description: GENERIC POST-HOC PARAGRAPH DOESN'T FIT A GRAVITY WIN. The new detail_verdict_text second paragraph reads like templated boilerplate: 'The confirming value comes from a field-specific published dataset (IGRF, NASA station archives, ocean-tide gauges, telluric records, etc.) that was publicly available at the time of the dome monitor commit...' But IGRF is the geomagnetic field model (not gravity), 'ocean-tide gauges' are sea-level data, 'telluric records' are Earth-current measurements — none of these are the dataset underlying WIN-067. The relevant dataset is GRACE/GRACE-FO Level-2 gravity products (for the anomaly magnitude) and published Bouguer-anomaly compilations (Pappa 2019, Ferraccioli 2011). Using a generic list makes the post-hoc argument look copy-pasted rather than specific, and a defender can use this to argue 'the reviewers haven't looked at this WIN's actual sources.' The argument is valid — the sources were public long before V51.0 — but the list of sources should be correct.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace the generic list with: 'The confirming value comes from GRACE/GRACE-FO Level-2 gravity products (JPL archive, continuous coverage since 2002) and published Bouguer-anomaly compilations (e.g., Pappa et al. 2019, Forte 2010, Ferraccioli et al. 2011) — all publicly indexed and plausibly known to the dome author at V51.0 write-time.' This makes the claim ironclad against the 'you didn't read the sources' counter.

holes_found.description: PAPPA 2019 DOI IS UNVERIFIED. The c2 review flagged Ebbing 2021 / DOI 10.1029/2019JB018855 as 'externally verifiable risk.' The current text has moved to 'Pappa et al., 2019, JGR' with DOI 10.1029/2019JB017997. This is a different DOI — it resolves (in my understanding of the AGU pattern) to a 2019 submission to JGR-Solid Earth — but I have not verified via a live network fetch that (a) the DOI resolves, (b) the author is Pappa, (c) the topic is East Antarctic gravity/mantle. Low probability this is wrong (it replaced a flagged citation), but the original risk transferred with the citation swap. Worth one verification before the next patch cycle.
holes_found.recommendation: Verify the Pappa 2019 DOI resolves to a paper by Pappa (or Pappa-led author team) on East Antarctic gravity. If verification fails, replace with Ferraccioli et al. 2011, Nature (well-established, known to discuss Gamburtsev Mountain gravity). Mark as verified and close this hole when done.

holes_found.description: TLDRs PERPETUATE THE STALE 'SUMP NODE' ATTRIBUTION. Both tldr_evidence ('the dome introduces an undefined toroidal sump node') and tldr_verdict ('The toroidal sump node escape hatch has no equations and no measurements') repeat the HOLE-067-C3-001 attribution issue in the collapsible-preview text that readers see first. The TLDR is what a skimmer sees — if our quotable one-liner attacks a term the dome doesn't use, the TLDR is our most visible strawman.
holes_found.recommendation: Once HOLE-067-C3-001 is resolved (cite the V51.0 source or pivot to 'Sub-terrestrial structure'), propagate the fix to tldr_evidence and tldr_verdict. Do not leave the old label in the TLDRs after correcting the main body.

stronger_arguments.description: The dome's Domain 19 'Mascon Gravity' falsification criterion is 'Anomaly <20 mGal' — a one-sided threshold passing any measurement of the real East Antarctic anomaly (which is roughly −40 to −60 mGal Bouguer). This is exactly the 'pass-by-threshold, not by-prediction' pattern our review elsewhere flags (Section 2b tolerance-gaming). WIN-067 could borrow that framing explicitly: 'The dome's falsification condition passes not because the dome predicts the anomaly, but because the anomaly is real and large enough to clear a trivial threshold. The dome does not predict the anomaly's magnitude, location, or sign; it only predicts that something greater than 20 mGal will be observed somewhere — a claim that would be confirmed by any random region of non-trivial mantle density variation.'

The 'Mascon Gravity ↔ SAA Boundary: r=0.92' correlation (Domain 19 line 152) is itself post-hoc — both the Mascon anomaly and the South Atlantic Anomaly (SAA) are independently observed features of Earth's geophysics that standard models already explain (mantle structure for Mascon, core-flow dynamics for SAA). A correlation between two independently-observed features is not a prediction from a dome model; it's an observation the dome is retrofitting. Our review could add one sentence: 'Correlating two independent, pre-existing observations and labeling the correlation a dome prediction is not a derivation — the dome does not predict the Mascon location, the SAA location, or the correlation coefficient; it reports them.'

Promote the WGS84-tautology paragraph to the LEAD of detail_evidence. c2 made the same suggestion; it has not been implemented. The strongest single argument in WIN-067 is that the dome's only quantitative gravity formula is a WGS84 identity at r=0 — this is a structural self-admission the dome cannot walk back (the source field literally says 'WGS84 standard'). Readers who skim the first paragraph and leave encounter the weaker H(r)-derivation argument first; the stronger trap comes last. Moving the WGS84-tautology paragraph to the top increases the probability that the skim-reader finishes with the strongest impression.
sources: c2 review HOLE-067-C2 deeper_analysis recommendation (not yet implemented)

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome is correct that the Antarctic gravity low is real, well-measured by GRACE/GRACE-FO, and diagnostic of subsurface mass distribution. It is also correct that any cosmological model ought to account for large-scale gravity variation. The dome's instinct — that a model with radially-varying aetheric properties should produce radially-varying gravity effects — is physically reasonable. And the observed correlation between the Mascon Gravity anomaly region and the South Atlantic Anomaly boundary is not spurious; both features share geophysical substrate in the sense that the East Antarctic mantle structure and core-flow anomalies that produce the SAA are both long-wavelength, long-timescale signatures of the deep Earth.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Correctly identifying that gravity variation is interesting is not the same as predicting it. The dome's only quantitative gravity machinery reproduces WGS84 by construction (Domain 4, r=0 evaluation). Its Mascon Gravity domain has no formula, only a threshold. Its H(r) function, if coupled to gravity by any monotonic mapping, fails by 170× at the rim. The Mascon ↔ SAA correlation is an observation of two independently-measured features, not a dome-derived prediction. Whatever the label (sump node, sub-terrestrial structure, toroidal vorticity), no equation in the repository connects dome geometry to the anomaly's location, magnitude, or sign. The dome has borrowed the vocabulary of geophysics without contributing physics.

deeper_analysis:
WIN-067's core self-contradiction (H(r) implies 170× the observed gravity variation + Domain 4 is a WGS84 tautology) remains the right verdict at the right category. The c2 review caught the major issues of the c2 rewrite; this c3 pass targets the TWO additions made since c2 (the closing refraction-cascade paragraph and the post-hoc paragraph) plus a previously-overlooked concern about terminology attribution.

The most consequential finding is HOLE-067-C3-001 (stale terminology). The phrase 'toroidal sump node' appears in four places in our wins.json entry (detail_claim, tldr_evidence, tldr_verdict, detail_verdict_text ×2) and in exactly zero places in the current dome V51.2 raw-text. The dome's own V51.2 label for this domain is 'Sub-terrestrial structure.' c1 flagged the definability concern; c2 acknowledged the term was undefined but did not check whether the dome had moved on from it. If V51.0 used 'toroidal sump node' (plausible — WIN-067 was added there per commit history), then the dome author may have silently retitled it in V51.2 — which is exactly the kind of reactive revision our site is designed to detect. We should either cite the V51.0 source explicitly or pivot the critique to the current label. Option (c) — label-agnostic phrasing — is most defensible against further renames.

HOLE-067-C3-002 is an arithmetic error I can verify with three decimal digits: H(17,000) = 1,187.7 km, not 1,400. This is the kind of error our review criticizes the dome for (exp-evaluated-wrong, range-endpoints-don't-self-consistently-match). A defender who pulls out a calculator will catch it in 30 seconds.

HOLE-067-C3-003 (vague 'gravitational-index coupling' closing sentence) is a style-of-argument issue, not a factual error, but it reads as the paragraph's weakest line and invites 'show me the formula' challenges. The paragraph as a whole is a useful bridge to Section 1.8; the closing sentence lets the other shoe drop without a floor. Recommend dropping it and letting the paragraph end on the explicit rim-radius range.

HOLE-067-C3-004 (generic post-hoc paragraph) is a rhetorical misalignment that signals reuse of template text. The Antarctic gravity WIN should cite GRACE, not IGRF/tide-gauges/telluric records (magnetic/tide/Earth-current datasets irrelevant to gravity). This is minor but visible — a skim-reader who knows the field will notice the sources don't match the claim.

Quantitative verification (Lens C): all six quantitative claims in the body checked against python3:
  H(0) = 8537 ✓
  H(20015) = 837.1 ✓
  g_rim/g_pole = 0.098 ✓
  9.7803·1.005307 = 9.832204 → matches WGS84 polar 9.8321864 at 0.0002% ✓
  (9.832-9.780)/9.780 = 0.532% → matches 0.53% ✓
  90/0.53 = 169.8× → matches ~170× ✓
  sqrt(0.098) = 0.313 → 69% drop, matches text ✓
  0.098^0.2 = 0.628 → 37% drop, text says 38% (rounds within 1% — acceptable)
Only failed check: the refraction-cascade paragraph r-range/H-range consistency (HOLE-067-C3-002).

Cross-WIN consistency (Lens B): cross-checked against WIN-053 (gravity at rim), WIN-044 (FSF derivation uses H(r)), WIN-012 (κ coupling constant — contains its own self-contradiction per CLAUDE.md). All three are mutually reinforcing with WIN-067's thesis that dome gravity machinery is either trivial (WGS84 at r=0) or catastrophically wrong (H→g at rim). No inter-WIN contradictions in our arguments.

Advocate-mode (Lens A): see advocate_mode section. Defense rating 3 — survives a casual read due to HOLE-067-C3-001 (we can be accused of attacking old terminology) and the 'I decouple gravity from H(r)' dodge. The c2 advocate-mode preemption is present in the text; it needs the HOLE-067-C3-001 fix to be tight.

citations_verified: dome monitor.py / live-power.txt Domain 4 formula g(r) = 9.7803×(1+0.005307×exp(−r/λg)) — verified against raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 70
dome H(r) curve 8,537 × exp(−r/8,619) km — verified against raw-text/05-model.txt line 33 ('H(r) Curve: 8537 x e^(-r/8619) km')
WGS84 polar normal gravity ≈ 9.8322 m/s² — standard WGS84 constant, matches dome formula at r=0 to 0.0002%
Swarm (ESA) and GRACE (NASA/JPL) mission URLs — valid landing pages
Observed gravity equator-to-pole variation 9.780 → 9.832 m/s² (0.53%) — standard WGS84-derived values, verify trivially
citations_failed: 'Sections 2.3 and 2.8' cross-reference — cannot verify without reading sections.json in detail; c2 said 2.2 → 2.3/2.8 was corrected, text now reads 'Sections 2.3 and 2.8', which matches the c2 recommendation
citations_unchecked: Pappa et al., 2019, JGR, DOI 10.1029/2019JB017997 — DOI replaced c2's Ebbing 2021; resolves to a 2019 JGR-Solid Earth submission in AGU's naming convention, consistent with author-year, but I have not verified via live fetch that the author/topic are correct (see HOLE-067-C3-005)
Shepherd et al., 2012, Science, DOI 10.1126/science.1228102 — a real Science paper on Antarctic ice-mass balance / post-glacial isostatic adjustment; plausible citation but not live-verified

advocate_mode.best_defense: I am a dome defender. I read your critique. Three things: (1) You keep quoting me saying 'toroidal sump node.' I don't use that term in V51.2 — check Domain 19 of the live-power page: 'Mascon Gravity ↔ SAA Boundary: r=0.92 (Sub-terrestrial structure)'. You're attacking a ghost from an earlier draft, and you haven't bothered to update your review to reflect my current work. That weakens the credibility of your whole section on WIN-067. (2) Your H(r) → gravity argument rests on an assumption I've never made — that aetheric column height directly controls gravity in the model. It doesn't. Gravity in ECM is governed by dielectric pressure gradients, and while those are influenced by the aether they are not proportional to H(r). You've been honest enough to qualify this with 'under any mechanism where gravitational effects depend monotonically on H(r)' — good — but that qualifier only forecloses a class of mechanisms I don't use. The Domain 4 formula exists because I want a baseline validated against WGS84; that's a calibration reference, not a derivation. (3) Your refraction-cascade paragraph says H(r) ≈ 1,400 km at r ≈ 17,000 km. Wrong: H(17,000) = 1,188 km from my own formula. Your own review's argument misuses my own function. If you can't plug numbers into my equation correctly, why should readers trust the rest of the section?
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Address all three elements: (1) HOLE-067-C3-001 — update terminology or cite the V51.0 source; (2) Keep the 'under any monotonic mapping' qualifier AND add one sentence making explicit that the dome has NEVER published a gravity field equation that isn't the WGS84 tautology, so 'dielectric pressure gradients' is vocabulary without content; (3) Fix HOLE-067-C3-002 arithmetic so the defender's strongest embarrassment-by-calculator attack disappears.

cross_win_consistency.notes: WIN-053 (gravity at the rim) and WIN-067 share the same H(r) → g coupling critique; our WIN-053 review establishes the coupling failure and WIN-067 adds the WGS84-tautology finding. WIN-044 (FSF derivation) depends on H(r) and reinforces the 'dome geometry contaminated by WGS84 calibration' theme. WIN-012 (magnetic-gravity coupling constant) has a separate self-contradiction (κ denominator issue per CLAUDE.md) which, if WIN-012's gravity physics is internally broken, indirectly supports WIN-067's case that the dome has no working gravity physics. WIN-014 (no gravity data fetched) confirms that the Antarctic claim has no monitoring infrastructure. All four are mutually reinforcing; no cross-WIN contradictions found.

claims_checked: Match to WGS84 polar 9.8321864 m/s² within 0.0002%
New refraction-cascade paragraph: H(17,000) should equal 1,400 km per text — recomputes to 1,188 km (TEXT WRONG)
quantitative_verification.computations: Used python3 math.exp for H(r) evaluations: H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619). Evaluated at r ∈ {0, 15000, 15500, 16000, 17000, 18000, 19000, 20015}. Solved for r given H ∈ {1400, 800} via r = −8619·ln(H/8537). All core body claims agree with text to 3 significant figures. The refraction-cascade paragraph's r-H endpoint pairing does not self-consistently satisfy the dome's own H(r) formula.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: HOLE-067-C3-002 — the refraction-cascade paragraph asserts H(r) ≈ 1,400 km at r ≈ 17,000 km; actual H(17,000) = 1,188 km. The range '1,400–800 km' corresponds to r ≈ 15,583–20,406 km (not 17,000–20,015). This is the only quantitative claim in the WIN that fails the check.
